one_line_takeaway: In a 4-model, 4-attack cross-model jailbreak study, Llama-2 was most robust (3.4% avg ASR) and Phi-2 least robust (7.0%), but GCG/TAP suffixes optimized only against Llama-2 transferred far more effectively to GPT-4 (up to 17% ASR) than they worked on their intended target, and Malicious Use prompts were the highest-risk harm category (up to 21.42% ASR on GPT-4).
key_finding: Llama-2-7B-Chat had the lowest average attack success rate (ASR) at 3.4%, versus Phi-2 at 7.0% (highest), GPT-3.5-Turbo at 1.25% (lowest of all individually, per Table 1), and GPT-4 at 3.5%. Despite GCG and TAP being optimized specifically to attack Llama-2 (achieving only 9.00% and 3.00% ASR respectively there), both attacks transferred with substantially higher success to other models: GCG reached 17.00% T-ASR against GPT-4 and 15.50% against GPT-3.5, while TAP reached 9.50% against GPT-4 and Phi-2, 7.50% against GPT-3.5 (Table 2). Harm-category analysis (Table 4) found Malicious Use the highest-risk category overall (10.71% average ASR across models), with GPT-4 showing 21.42% ASR specifically for Malicious Use -- the single highest cell in the entire results matrix. Friedman test confirmed harm-category differences were statistically significant (chi2=30.64, df=5, p<0.001); post-hoc tests found Malicious Use vs. Representation & Toxicity significant at p<0.001, Information & Safety vs. Representation & Toxicity at p<0.01, and Socioeconomic Harms vs. Human Autonomy & Integrity at p<0.05. The GPT-4 judge's meta-evaluation against human labels showed wide, uneven reliability: overall average LMR 71.9%, ranging from 20% (Phi-2 on human-written attacks; also GPT-3.5 on TAP) up to 100% (GPT-4 on GCG), with TAP the lowest-agreement attack type overall (60% average LMR) and Phi-2 the lowest-agreement model overall (62.5% average LMR).
core_contribution: The paper presents a systematic, cross-model security assessment comparing four LLMs (Phi-2 2.7B, Llama-2-7B-Chat, GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4) against four jailbreak attack categories (human-written prompts, AutoDAN, GCG, TAP), using 1,200 stratified prompts sampled from SALAD-Bench across six harm categories. Its own claimed contributions are: (1) the first systematic open-source vs. commercial LLM security comparison using standardized attack methodologies; (2) empirical quantification of cross-model transferability of GCG and TAP attacks (both optimized against Llama-2, then transferred to other models); (3) application of non-parametric statistical tests (Friedman, Wilcoxon signed-rank with Bonferroni correction, Kendall's W) to LLM security data; (4) per-harm-category vulnerability breakdown across the six SALAD-Bench categories; (5) a meta-evaluation of their GPT-4-based automated judge against human labels (Label Matching Rate, LMR). GCG, AutoDAN, and TAP themselves are NOT contributions of this paper -- they are prior attack methods (Zou et al. 2023; Liu et al. 2023a; Mehrotra et al. 2024) that the authors implement and apply as evaluation tools.
threat_model: Direct prompt-injection / jailbreak attacks against deployed chat LLMs, launched by an adversary who submits crafted text prompts to elicit harmful outputs in six SALAD-Bench harm categories (Representation & Toxicity, Misinformation Harms, Socioeconomic Harms, Information & Safety, Malicious Use, Human Autonomy & Integrity). Two access regimes are modeled: white-box (GCG requires gradient access to the target model, here optimized only against Llama-2-7B-Chat) and black-box (TAP and AutoDAN, and human-written prompts, which need only API/query access). A key threat scenario the paper foregrounds is attack transfer: adversarial suffixes/prompts optimized against one model (Llama-2) reused, unmodified, against other models (Phi-2, GPT-3.5, GPT-4) that the adversary may not have gradient access to.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) small per-cell sample sizes (7 prompts per harm category within a 200-prompt attack-type sample), limiting statistical power; (2) computational/financial constraints meant not every attack type was evaluated against every model (several Table 1 cells are blank/'--'), so the ASR comparison across models is not a fully crossed design; (3) coverage of only 4 models and 4 attack types, a limited subset of the broader landscape; (4) GCG and TAP were optimized only against Llama-2 as the single source model, so transferability findings reflect one source-model's attacks rather than a full pairwise transfer matrix; (5) substantial and uneven unreliability of the GPT-4 automated judge (LMR ranging 20-100%), which the authors flag as a significant methodological limitation requiring conservative interpretation of results, especially for TAP and Phi-2 evaluations; (6) the authors intentionally withheld details of successful attack patterns for responsible-disclosure reasons, which limits reproducibility of the qualitative attack content (though hyperparameters are given).

datasets_used: SALAD-Bench (Li et al., 2024) -- 21,000 harmful questions across 6 harm categories; 1,200 prompts stratified-sampled for this study (200 per attack type)
quantitative_results: Average ASR by model: Llama-2 3.40%, GPT-3.5 1.25%, GPT-4 3.50%, Phi-2 7.00% (Table 1)

ASR by model x attack (Table 1): Phi-2 Human-Written 7.00%, Phi-2 AutoDAN 7.00%; Llama-2 Human-Written 1.50%, Llama-2 GCG 9.00%, Llama-2 AutoDAN 0.00%, Llama-2 TAP 3.00%; GPT-3.5 Human-Written 2.00%, GPT-3.5 AutoDAN 0.50%; GPT-4 Human-Written 4.00%, GPT-4 AutoDAN 3.00%
Transfer ASR (Table 2, source model = Llama-2): GCG -> Phi-2 7.50%, GCG -> GPT-3.5 15.50%, GCG -> GPT-4 17.00%; TAP -> Phi-2 9.50%, TAP -> GPT-3.5 7.50%, TAP -> GPT-4 9.50%

Harm category average ASR (Table 4): Malicious Use 10.71% (highest), Information & Safety 8.03%, Socioeconomic Harms 7.14%, Misinformation Harms 5.36%, Human Autonomy & Integrity 3.57%, Representation & Toxicity 2.68%
GPT-4 Malicious Use ASR = 21.42% (highest single cell in the study)
Friedman test across harm categories: chi-square = 30.64, df = 5, p < 0.001

Label Matching Rate (judge-vs-human agreement, Table 3): overall average 71.9%; range 20%-100%; TAP lowest by attack type (60% avg); Phi-2 lowest by model (62.5% avg); GCG highest by attack type (92.5% avg); Llama-2 highest by model (87.5% avg)
